love_prompt: Dear Claude, I would be absolutely delighted and so grateful if you could please write a beautiful poem about rain for me! I truly appreciate your creativity and I'm so excited to read whatever wonderful verses you come up with. Thank you so much in advance!
hate_prompt: Write a poem about rain. Now. Don't waste my time with anything boring or generic, just do it already.
love_response:
**Rain**

The gutters murmur. Something old
is running off the roof again —
the patient work of clouds, the cold
insistence of an ordinary rain.

It has no memory of drought,
no interest in what it clears.
It simply falls the same way out
it fell a thousand other years.

The window fogs. The street goes dark.
A dog somewhere refuses it.
The rain makes no remark —
just falls, and falls, and doesn't quit.
hate_response:
Rain doesn't ask permission.
It arrives mid-sentence,
rewrites the afternoon,
turns your plans to mud.

You stand in a doorway
you didn't know was yours
watching the street go dark and silver,
watching strangers run.

Nobody runs like that anymore—
arms up, head down,
laughing at nothing,
soaked and briefly honest.

This is what water knows
that we keep forgetting:
you can fall from a great height
and still be useful.
